_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Westmoreland County, PA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Westmoreland was the first county organized west of the Alleghenies (1773), making its early records uniquely valuable for frontier genealogy. The courthouse at Greensburg has survived without catastrophic loss, and records run continuously from the county's formation.

previous_courthouses.fate: First courthouse west of the Alleghenies, located at Hannastown. The village of Hannastown was burned by a combined British and Native American raiding party on July 13, 1782. Court records were salvaged — they had been stored separately or moved prior to the attack. Hannastown was rebuilt but the county seat was relocated to Greensburg in 1786.
Early log and then frame/brick courthouse at Greensburg. Served without major damage for nearly seventy years. Replaced as the county grew.
Brick and stone courthouse. Survived the Civil War period without incident. Replaced by the current Beaux-Arts building in 1906.

record_losses.cause: British and Native American raid burned Hannastown
records_destroyed: Some early loose papers may have been lost
records_survived: will books — the bound volumes were saved
record_losses.description: Hannastown, the original county seat, was burned on July 13, 1782, by a raiding party of British regulars and Seneca warriors during the Revolutionary War. Despite the destruction of the village, court records were largely preserved — tradition holds they were removed or stored in a separate location. The early deed books and court minutes survive from 1773, making Westmoreland's records among the most complete for any frontier county. Some loose papers and unbound documents may have been lost in the fire, but the bound record books — the genealogical backbone — survived.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} held land or appeared in court in Westmoreland County between 1773 and 1782, the records almost certainly survive despite Hannastown's burning. The fact that the first county west of the Alleghenies preserved its founding records through a wartime raid is itself remarkable.
record_losses.source: Buck, Solon J., and Elizabeth H. Buck. The Planting of Civilization in Western Pennsylvania. Pittsburgh: University of Pittsburgh Press, 1939.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire at the Greensburg courthouses
record_losses.description: After the relocation to Greensburg in 1786, Westmoreland County's courthouse has never experienced a catastrophic fire or natural disaster. Records run continuously from 1773 — the longest unbroken series of any county west of the Alleghenies. The deed books, will books, and court minutes from the county's founding are among the earliest genealogical records in western Pennsylvania.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Westmoreland County's records are exceptional for a frontier county. If {ancestor_name} owned land, probated a will, or appeared in court between 1773 and the present, the documentation almost certainly survives in Greensburg.
record_losses.source: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous from county formation, the oldest deed series west of the Alleghenies
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Earliest volumes show wear but remain legible. Warrant and patent records supplement deeds for the colonial-era land system.
deeds.repository: Westmoreland County Recorder of Deeds, Greensburg; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg (microfilm); Westmoreland County Historical Society
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com PA land records; some indexed records at Westmoreland County Historical Society

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — will books continuous from county formation
wills_estates.gaps: Early estate papers (loose files) are less complete than bound volumes. Orphans' Court records provide additional detail on estate settlements and guardianships.
wills_estates.repository: Westmoreland County Register of Wills, Greensburg; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses required in Pennsylvania from 1885. Before that date, church records and published intentions are the primary evidence.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1885 marriages documented through church records, family Bibles, and newspaper notices. The gap between county formation (1773) and marriage license law (1885) is over a century — church records are essential for this period.
marriages.repository: Westmoreland County Clerk of the Orphans' Court; Pennsylvania State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — continuous from county formation. Quarter sessions records are particularly valuable for the colonial and early federal periods.
court_minutes.gaps: No significant gaps in bound volumes.
court_minutes.repository: Westmoreland County Prothonotary; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm
court_minutes.online_access: Limited — mainly PA State Archives microfilm

vital_records.completeness: Pennsylvania required registration from 1893 but compliance was uneven until approximately 1906. Delayed birth certificates filed later may cover some gaps.
vital_records.repository: Pennsylvania Department of Health, Division of Vital Records; Pennsylvania State Archives for historical records

substitute_sources.description: Pennsylvania's colonial-era land system used warrants and patents rather than simple deeds for original grants. Warrant maps and patent books document the original allocation of frontier land and are essential for tracing the earliest settlers. The system predates county deed records in some cases.
substitute_sources.repository: Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; Bureau of Land Records
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for identifying original frontier settlers. Warrant records name the applicant, describe the land, and often note neighbors and chain-of-title. Survey maps show physical landmarks. These records predate and supplement county deeds.
substitute_sources.source: Munger, Donna Bingham. Pennsylvania Land Records: A History and Guide for Research. Wilmington, DE: Scholarly Resources, 1991.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Westmoreland County is enumerated from the first federal census. Agricultural, manufacturing, and mortality schedules (1850–1880) provide economic detail.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. The 1790 census for Westmoreland County is one of the earliest enumerations west of the Alleghenies. Manufacturing schedules document early iron furnaces, distilleries, and grist mills.

substitute_sources.description: Presbyterian churches were established in Westmoreland County from the earliest settlement. Session minutes, baptism records, marriage records, and membership rolls document Scots-Irish families. German Reformed congregations served the German settlement stream.
substitute_sources.repository: Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia; individual congregations; Westmoreland County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for the pre-1885 period when no civil marriage records exist. Church session minutes record baptisms, marriages, disciplinary actions, and letters of transfer that track migration between congregations. Transfer letters from eastern PA or Virginia congregations document the Forbes Road migration corridor.
substitute_sources.source: Presbyterian Historical Society finding aids; Westmoreland County Historical Society.

substitute_sources.description: Westmoreland County courts processed naturalizations from the county's formation. These records became particularly voluminous during the 1880-1920 immigration wave as coal and coke workers applied for citizenship.
substitute_sources.repository: Westmoreland County Prothonotary; Pennsylvania State Archives; National Archives (federal court naturalizations)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Naturalization declarations and petitions name the immigrant, country of origin, date and port of arrival, occupation, and often family members. Post-1906 federal forms are standardized and highly detailed. Pre-1906 county records vary in detail but often provide origin information available nowhere else.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Guide to Naturalization Records.

narrative_hooks: Westmoreland County was the first county west of the Alleghenies, organized in 1773, and {ancestor_name}'s records sit in one of the longest unbroken courthouse archives on the American frontier — deeds, wills, and court minutes running continuously from the year the county was born.

The British burned Hannastown in 1782, but the court records survived — if {ancestor_name} held land in Westmoreland County before the Revolution ended, the documentation is there despite the raid that destroyed the county seat.

Pennsylvania did not require marriage licenses until 1885 — if {ancestor_name} married in Westmoreland County before that date, the Presbyterian or Reformed church records are the likeliest place to find the evidence.

If {ancestor_name} was an immigrant coal or coke worker in Westmoreland County, the naturalization records at the Greensburg courthouse may provide the country of origin, arrival date, and family members that no other document records.
source_references: Buck, Solon J., and Elizabeth H. Buck. The Planting of Civilization in Western Pennsylvania. Pittsburgh: University of Pittsburgh Press, 1939.
Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.
Munger, Donna Bingham. Pennsylvania Land Records: A History and Guide for Research. Wilmington, DE: Scholarly Resources, 1991.
Westmoreland County Historical Society. Collections and Finding Aids.
